title: Eros the Bittersweet
description: An essay in the form of a classical argument, Eros the Bittersweet begins with a simple observation: that desire requires distance. Carson traces the triangular structure of longing through Greek lyric poetry, Plato, and the invention of the alphabet itself. To reach toward something is already to feel its absence. Love, she argues, is not a feeling but a lack made visible. The book does not resolve this. It holds the ache open.

title: The Ballad of Sexual Dependency
caption: 700 photographs of intimacy, violence, tenderness, and dependency among Goldin's circle over a decade.

title: Are You Looking Up (Live)
caption: Mk.gee live at the Brooklyn Paramount (Sun, Sep 29-2024)

title: All About Love: New Visions
description: Hooks opens with a question no one seems to want to answer directly: what is love, actually. Not the feeling, not the fantasy but the practice. Drawing on M. Scott Peck's definition of love as the will to extend oneself for another's spiritual growth, she argues that most of what we call love is really need, fear, or control dressed up in softer language. The book is both a diagnosis and an instruction. It insists that love is a choice made repeatedly, in the ordinary, when it is inconvenient.

caption: Zine expressing a deeply personal message where "home" is not a place, but a person.

title: Cruel Optimism
description: A cruel optimism exists when the thing you desire is an obstacle to your own flourishing. Berlant is writing about politics and precarity, but the argument lands just as hard on love. The attachments we maintain not because they are good for us but because they organize our sense of what a life should feel like. To let go of the fantasy is to lose the structure entirely. The book sits with that difficulty without flinching. It does not ask you to stop wanting. It asks you to look clearly at what wanting costs.

title: Art/Life One Year Performance
caption: Two artists tied by an 8-foot rope for one year. Not allowed to touch.

title: On Earth We're Briefly Gorgeous
description: A letter from a son to a mother who cannot read. Vuong writes about tenderness the way most people can only feel it, in the body, in the specific. The book moves between Vietnam and Connecticut, between war and a tobacco field, between a boy figuring out what he is and a man trying to explain it to the woman who made him. Love here is not romantic. It is the harder kind. The kind that holds history inside it and asks you to look at all of it.

title: Fuck You
caption: Rare occasion of Nicole's middle finger (Mon, Jan 1-2024)

title: Bluets
description: 240 propositions about the color blue. Also about heartbreak, about a friend rendered quadriplegic, about desire and its aftermath. Nelson numbers each one. They do not resolve into an argument. They accumulate the way grief does, circling back, touching different parts of the same wound. The book is a formal experiment that works because the form is the feeling. You can open it anywhere and be inside it immediately.

title: A Lover's Discourse: Fragments
description: An index of the lover's interior life. Barthes takes the private language of being in love and treats it with the full seriousness of a linguist, a philosopher, someone who believes that what happens inside us when we love someone is worth studying as carefully as any text. The entries are alphabetical: absence, adorable, waiting, the other's body. It is a book about how love makes us speak and what that speech reveals. Reading it feels less like being analyzed and more like being recognized.

title: The Diary of Anaïs Nin, Vol. 1
description: Written in real time across the early 1930s in Paris, this is a record of a woman's interior life at full intensity. Nin writes about desire without shame, about her relationship with Henry Miller, about what it feels like to want someone and to be known by them. The diary is not neat. It is contradictory, obsessive, self-aware about its own obsessiveness. She is studying herself the way you study someone you love, which is to say: with complete attention and no certainty of ever fully understanding.

title: The Second Sex
description: The foundational argument is that woman has been defined historically as Other, as the negative term in relation to man. But the sections on love are where the book becomes personal. De Beauvoir describes what happens to a woman who makes love the center of her life, how that choice is conditioned, how it can become a kind of erasure. She is not against love. She is trying to describe what genuine love between equals might actually require, and how rarely the conditions for it exist.

title: Their Eyes Were Watching God
description: Janie Crawford has three marriages across the span of the novel. The first two teach her what love is not. The third is the real thing: messy, physical, equal, ruinous in the end. Hurston wrote this in seven weeks and it shows, in the best way. The prose is alive. She is not interested in love as a concept. She is interested in what it feels like inside the body of a Black woman in Florida in the 1930s who refuses to stop looking for something real.
